data.title: DigitalBridge: Sustained Collapse Sows Uncertainty About Digital Infrastructure Assets
data.description:
DigitalBridge Group, Inc. (NYSE:DBRG) is down 37% year-to-date to cap off a torrid start to 2025. This held the promise of a ramp in fee-earning equity under management ("FEEUM") with DBRG pushing to expand this to $40 billion at the end of the year, up at least $6 billion from FEEUM of $36 billion as of the end of the fiscal 2024 fourth quarter. The asset manager raised $9 billion in new fee-paying capital in 2024, exceeding its target to raise $7 billion, as demand for digital infrastructure assets and businesses continued to ramp up during the year. DBRG's investment focus on digital infrastructure as an asset class has formed the core part of its investment profile, but total returns for the stock have been dire on the back of consistent losses.

DBRG generated fourth quarter revenue of $66.17 million, down 81.1% over its year-ago comp and a miss by $27.77 million on consensus estimates. The asset manager declared a peppercorn cash dividend of $0.01 per share, $0.04 per share annually for a 0.56% dividend yield. FEEUM grew by 8% year-over-year in 2024. Critically, DBRG at its current $1.32 billion market cap is materially down versus its historical trading range, reflecting a macro environment where its core appeal to institutional investors, including insurance companies, sovereign wealth funds, pension funds, and family offices, is markedly dimmed on capital flight to haven and highly liquid assets.

Risks, Growth, And The Trendline

We saw the US 10-year Treasury yield collapse in recent days following "Liberation Day" as capital rushed towards the asset class to act as a hedge against the material level of uncertainty currently faced by the US economy. There have been different tranches of tariffs already implemented, from auto tariffs, liberation day tariffs, and steel tariffs, with some further tariffs also pending, including a levy on Chinese-built ships docking in US ports. The downstream impact of this is undoubtedly inflationary, perhaps only slightly countered by the collapse in oil prices.

The Atlanta Fed GDPNow model's latest estimate for the first quarter of 2025 GDP growth is negative 2.8%, down from positive economic growth north of 2% as recently as January. This comes as the one-year US inflation outlook in March hit 5% according to the University of Michigan Survey of Consumers. The five-year implied inflation expectation came in at 4.3%, up 80 basis points versus the prior month. Hence, the core risk for DBRG and the core driver of the collapse in the asset manager's valuation year-to-date is the confluence of what has been historically parallel factors of inflation and recession, the combination of both will be extremely destructive to capital raising efforts, and forms the core metric for both DBRG bulls and bears to monitor their position.

DBRG is now trading at its lowest-ever level, even with its FEEUM at the highest it has ever been for the digital infrastructure alternative asset manager. The market is looking towards a potentially recessionary macro environment, in aggregate with rising inflation and a paralyzed Fed, as reasons to sell DBRG and dampen expectations that the company will be able to meet its target for 2025 FEEUM growth. However, bulls should continue to anchor their hopes on DBRG's aggregate investments and businesses with long-term contracted cash flows, investment-grade customers, and predictable yields across its verticals from data centers, small cell networks, edge infrastructure, macro cell towers, and fiber networks.

DBRG earned fee revenue of $330 million during the fourth quarter on a trailing 12-month basis, up $27 million sequentially, with fee-related earnings hitting $107 million during the quarter, up $9 million sequentially. A potential reversal of this could be in the cards, lining up DBRG for what would possibly be its first decline in FRE if the US were to fall into a recession and a broad appeal for digital infrastructure and liquidity were to materially fall in exchange for US treasuries. While I held a position in the commons, this was closed this month, with the preferreds offering a safer investment option with a markedly higher yield. DBRG has three preferreds: the 7.125% Series H (NYSE:DBRG.PR.H), 7.15% Series I (NYSE:DBRG.PR.I), and the 7.125% Series J (NYSE:DBRG.PR.J). These like other non-investment grade fixed-income securities, have also seen their respective values fall drastically in response to the radically uncertain macro environment to offer yields in the range of 7.2% to 8%. For investors looking to maintain exposure to DBRG, they're the better choice. However, they could see their value fall further if consumer inflation ramps up from here and the Fed faces a recession while being forced to keep rates elevated.

Performance displayed represents past performance, which is no guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that when shares are redeemed, they may be worth more or less than original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For up-to-date fund performance, including performance current to the most recent month end, please visit our website at Guggenheim Investments. Load performance reflects maximum sales charges or contingent deferred sales charges (CDSC) as applicable. A Class has a maximum sales charge of 3.00%.

The referenced fund is offered in multiple share classes. Please read the prospectus for information on fees, expenses and holding periods that may apply to each class.

  1. Inception date of benchmark return is that of the fund’s oldest share class.
  2. The Net Expense Ratio reflects a contractual fee waiver by the Adviser through February 1, 2025, to limit the ordinary operating expenses of the fund.
  3. The Adjusted Expense Ratio (“Adjusted”) is based on the Net Expense Ratio, but excludes certain investment expenses, such as interest expense from borrowings and repurchase agreements and dividend expense from investments on short sales.
  4. Benchmark-S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index.

Unless otherwise noted, data is as of 12.31.2024. Data subject to change. Partial year returns are cumulative. Returns reflect the reinvestment of dividends. The referenced index is unmanaged and not available for direct investment. Index performance does not reflect transaction costs, fees, or expenses. Index data source: FundStation.

Performance Review

  • The Fund (Institutional Class) returned 2.01 percent for the fourth quarter compared with the S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index benchmark return of 2.29 percent. Performance was driven by credit selection, particularly in the consumer noncyclical and technology sectors, as well as in CCC-rated credits. Selection in the consumer cyclical sector was the period’s main detractor.
  • For the full year, high absolute returns for the portfolio and benchmark were driven by the high floating rate coupon. The Fund returned 7.17 percent versus the benchmark return of 9.05 percent. Performance was primarily impacted by idiosyncratic credit issues in the consumer and communications sectors. These credits are stressed/distressed issuers within the Fund for which we view ultimate recoveries as higher than current trading levels. Contributors to relative performance were credit selection in and an underweight to the technology sector, as well as selection in the nonrated segment.

Strategy and Positioning

  • Barring any major macroeconomic shocks, we expect the 2024 trends to persist in 2025 and that loan returns will be driven by high all-in coupon, with the potential for continued modest tightening of credit spreads and a similar, but not spiking, level of credit losses. Spread tightening could be driven by a continued supply/demand imbalance from lower net new loan issuance combined with continued strong collateralized loan obligation (CLO) demand.
  • Defaults have ticked higher due to higher interest burdens, but average issuer earnings have continued to grow. As such, the default rate stands mildly above the long-term average at 3.4 percent as of year-end 2024. Tail risk persists, but we expect defaults and losses will remain manageable.
  • The platform’s strategy continues to focus on bottom-up credit selection and loss avoidance. We continue to believe this strategy will drive outperformance across a credit cycle.
  • In terms of positioning, we are continuing to use the market strength to trim risk, and we remain selective in adding new names to the platform. Our new issue approval rate for the full year 2024 was 19 percent, 61 of 326 net of incremental term loans and refinancing transactions for existing credits. We are also monitoring our portfolios for any outsized impact from the change in administration.

Market Review

  • The S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index returned 2.29 percent for the fourth quarter, bringing the full year return to 9.05 percent. Though below 2023’s post-Global Financial Crisis record, this was the second-best return for the Index in the past eight years. Given the rate volatility in the fourth quarter, loans outpaced higher-duration credit for the quarter and the full year, with the ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Index return essentially flat for the quarter and returning 8.20 percent for the full year 2024.
  • Carry continues to drive loan returns, with interest income contributing 2.2 percent for the fourth quarter and 9.7 percent for the year. While still high compared to historical standards—interest income contribution has averaged 6.2 percent over the past 10 years— this was below 2023 levels due to the combination of base rate cuts as well as record repricing and refinancing activity reducing nominal spreads over the course of the year.
  • The technical backdrop continued to favor loan issuers in the fourth quarter. Strong demand from both CLOs and retail investors, combined with low net new loan issuance, led to another repricing wave. December’s repricing volume of $153 billion hit a new monthly record, bringing the annual total to a record $757 billion, or 55 percent of the total market. Combined with $260 billion of refinancings, the index average nominal spread has declined 25 basis points to 375 basis points this year, saving issuers $3.5 billion in annual interest payments.
  • On the demand front, CLO issuance ended the year at a new annual record of $201 billion, exceeding the prior record of $187 billion in 2021. Combined with $9.6 billion of net retail fund inflows, total loan demand surplus of $191 billion this year was the highest on record.

Sources: S&P LCD, S&P UBS LL.

Bps (basis points): One basis point is equal to 0.01%. Carry: The difference between the cost of financing an asset and the interest received on that asset. Duration: A measure of the sensitivity of the price of a bond to a change in interest rates. In general, the higher the duration the more the bond’s price will change with interest rate movements. Spread: Spread refers to the difference between yields on different debt instruments and is used to understand the relative value and risks of different debt instruments.

Risk Considerations. This fund may not be suitable for all investors.

Investments in floating rate senior secured syndicated bank loans and other floating rate securities involve special types of risks, including credit rate risk, interest rate risk, liquidity risk and prepayment risk.

The fund’s market value will change in response to interest rate changes and market conditions among other factors. In general, bond prices rise when interest rates fall and vice versa.

The fund’s exposure to high yield securities may subject the fund to greater volatility.

When market conditions are deemed appropriate, the fund will leverage to the full extent permitted by its investment policies and restrictions and applicable law. Leveraging will exaggerate the effect on net asset value of any increase or decrease in the market value of the fund’s portfolio.

The fund may invest in derivative instruments, which may be more volatile and less liquid, increasing the risk of loss when compared to traditional securities. Certain of the derivative instruments are also subject to the risks of counterparty default and adverse tax treatment.

Instruments and strategies (such as borrowing transactions and reverse repurchase agreements) may provide leveraged exposure to a particular investment, which will magnify any gains or losses on those investments. Investments in reverse repurchase agreements and synthetic instruments (such as synthetic collateralized debt obligations) expose the fund to the many of the same risks as investments in derivatives.

The fund’s investments in other investment vehicles subject the fund to those risks and expenses affecting the investment vehicle.

The fund’s investments in foreign securities carry additional risks when compared to U.S. securities, due to the impact of diplomatic, political or economic developments in the country in question (investments in emerging markets securities are generally subject to an even greater level of risks).

The fund’s investments in real estate securities subject the fund to the same risks as direct investments in real estate, which is particularly sensitive to economic downturns.

  • The fund’s investments in restricted securities may involve financial and liquidity risk.
  • The fund is subject to active trading risks that may increase volatility and impact its ability to achieve its investment objective.
  • You may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares.
  • It is important to note that the fund is not guaranteed by the U.S. government.
  • Please read the prospectus for more detailed information regarding these and other risks.

Index Definition. The S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index is designed to mirror the investable universe of the US$-denominated leveraged loan market. The ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Index is an unmanaged index that tracks the performance of U.S. dollar-denominated, below investment-grade rated corporate debt publicly issued in the U.S. domestic market.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Musk's Reported Plea To Reverse Tariffs Falls Flat
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Musk also went after trade adviser Peter Navarro’s Tesla comments. (0:16) Stocks still volatile as hopes for fast tariff exceptions and resolutions fade. (1:29) USTR Greer spooks bulls. (2:12)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, Tesla CEO and White House adviser Elon Musk unsuccessfully made personal appeals to President Donald Trump to reverse the sweeping new tariffs. That’s according to The Washington Post report.

Musk has voiced his disagreement with new tariffs, using social media platform X to criticize White House trade adviser Peter Navarro and posting a video featuring economist Milton Friedman, who argued the benefits of international trade cooperation.

Today, Musk labeled Navarro “truly a moron” and “dumber than a sack of bricks” for claiming on CNBC that Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) is not a car “manufacturer,” but a car “assembler,” with components from other countries. Musk said Tesla has the most American-made cars, citing a study by Cars.com (which also included Canada in that definition).

Elon Musk's brother, Kimbal Musk, a fellow Tesla board member, wrote: “Who would have thought that Trump was actually the most high tax American President in generations. Through his tariff strategy, Trump has implemented a structural, permanent tax on the American consumer.”

While Trump himself has been steadfast in his recent statement that the goal is for the U.S. to eliminate trade deficits with every nation it trades with, Wall Street still looks hostages to how advisers and cabinet members see the White House moving toward the goals.

In today’s trading, stocks got off to a rip-roaring start after avoiding a Black Monday scenario in the previous session.

The S&P (SP500) (SPY) climbed as high as 4% after Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said about 70 countries have contacted the White House seeking to start trade. He also said that countries that did not escalate the trade war will get priority in trade talks.

"If we put up a tariff wall, the ultimate goal would be to bring jobs back to the U.S. But in the meantime, we will be collecting substantial tariffs," Bessent said. He likened the tariffs to a "melting ice cube...because you’re taking in the revenues as the manufacturing facilities are built in the U.S., and there should be some level of symmetry between the taxes we begin taking in with the new industry from the payroll taxes as the tariffs decline."

But the market gains were cut in half after U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer testified before the Senate Finance Committee and downplayed the possibility of a quick resolution.

Greer stressed that Trump has been clear that there won’t be tariff exemptions in the near term. They go into effect on Wednesday.

He said negotiations would be country-by-country, adding: “Our large and persistent trade deficit has been over 30 years in the making, and it will not be resolved overnight.”

Over in the bond market, the action continued to flout the flight-to-safety narrative. Yields are up again, and the 10-year yield (US10Y) is above 4.2%, while the 2-year (US2Y) is above 3.8%.

Apollo Asset Management economists Torsten Slok says the unusual selloff in bonds may be linked to the unwinding of the "basis trade," a complex strategy used by hedge funds.

The basis trade involves betting on the price difference between Treasury securities and their corresponding futures contracts. Hedge funds often use significant leverage, sometimes up to 100 times, to profit from the convergence of these prices as futures contracts approach expiration. With around $800 billion invested in the basis trade, it constitutes a large portion of the $2 trillion outstanding in prime brokerage balances.

Experts worry that the trade poses risks, especially in the event of a market shock. Highly leveraged hedge fund positions in Treasury securities could be rapidly unwound, creating instability. Such unwinding would place immense pressure on broker-dealers, potentially disrupting liquidity in Treasury markets and repo funding.

Among active stocks, several Apple (AAPL) stores have seen instances of “panic buying” of iPhones amid worries that prices could rise due to tariffs. Bloomberg said stores across the U.S. were “filled with customers” at levels akin to the holiday season.

“Almost every customer asked me if prices were going to go up soon,” an unidentified worker said.

And El Pollo Loco (LOCO) disclosed that it received an unsolicited, non-binding indication of interest from Biglari Capital Corp. to acquire the rest of the company that it does not already own. Biglari holds through various entities about 4.5 million shares of El Pollo Loco, or about 15.1% of the company.

The El Pollo Loco’s board of directors is in the process of carefully evaluating the proposal in consultation with its independent financial and legal advisors to determine the course of action it believes is in the best interests of the company and shareholders.

In other news of note, the U.S. Justice Department recently notified employees that the regulator was scrapping a unit focused on crypto enforcement, aligning with the Trump administration's push for deregulation.

Fortune said the National Cryptocurrency Enforcement Unit was disbanded "effective immediately," according to a memo from U.S. Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche.

He added that the DOJ is "not a digital assets regulator."

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, while the attention has been on the big names and major averages, small-caps have been hit hard as well by the tariff selloff, with the Russell 2000 benchmark (RTY) (IWM) tipping into a bear market.

Morgan Stanley expects to see further underperformance of small-cap stocks relative to large-caps, partially because they have a higher exposure to macro uncertainty and because analysts have been pulling down earnings estimates.

“That said, small-cap opportunities are rich under the surface,” strategist Michael Wilson argues.

Companies with positive ROE and Momentum have performed well within the small-cap space, both on a historical basis and during 2024 through Q1 2025. Morgan Stanley screened for small-cap stocks with positive 12-month Momentum and ROE with either an Overweight or Equal-weight rating from its analysts.

Among the names are Brinker (EAT), Group 1 Automotive (GPI), Cadence Bank (CADE), Hims & Hers (HIMS) and Moog (MOG.A).

Check out all the names.

data.title: Big Yielders Down Big: 3 Top Ideas (REITs, BDCs And CEFs)
data.description:
The market is currently offering a "flash sale" on select big-yield opportunities (i.e. those offering 10% yields or more). This doesn't happen often, so let's review these temporary "sale prices" to make sure we're getting quality and not junk. In this report, we review three top big-yield opportunities that are currently down big (including a BDC, a CEF and a REIT) and then conclude with our strong opinion on investing.

Overview:

Unless you are living under a rock, you already know the market is down big, courtesy (in large part) to President Donald Trump's newly announced tariffs (and the economic repercussions).

But the turmoil has just now spread into the big-yield investment space (e.g. BDCs, REITs and CEFs) as fear spikes (VIX), credit spreads have widened, and usually safe big yields are now trading at "flash sale" prices.

For example, look at what just happened to big-yield business development companies as measured by the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD), comprised of a group of BDCs and with an aggregate yield of around 12%.

And the carnage was similar for big-yield bond closed-end funds as measured by one of the industry's leaders, PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI), which currently yields more than 14%.

And as a third example, check out the wreckage in the mortgage REIT (real estate investment trust) space, another big-yield group (13.3%) that normally trades fairly flat but just sold off hard.

As per the charts above, not only have prices plummeted over the last few trading sessions, but the yields have spiked (as price falls, yield mathematically rises, all else equal).

And the reason for the selloff among big-yield opportunities is fear (as measured by the VIX (VIX) for example), and because credit spreads have widened (see charts below). Credit spreads are a measure of perceived risk among high-yield debt.

Why Big Yield?

We get it. You’re at a stage in life where generating steady income from your investments is more important than suffering the slings and arrows of outrageous volatility (such as the amount we have yet again been experiencing recently—which may continue—and will absolutely rear its ugly head again at some point in the future).

By focusing instead on steady, big income payments, many investors sleep much better at night. After all, why sweat the dramatic short-term ups and downs so long as those big steady income payments (often in excess of 10%) keep rolling in.

Is 10% Plus Really Safe?

But how safe is a 10%-plus yield? Really? Obviously, there must be some risks and trade-offs for focusing on steady big income instead of volatile long-term price appreciation potential—right?

The conventional argument is for investors to focus on total returns (i.e. dividends, as if they were reinvested, plus price appreciation). But a lot of people are not reinvesting the dividends. Instead, they’re spending some of them to cover living expenses, retirement account RMDs (required minimum distributions) and simply because they don’t want to live and die (financially) by whatever lame-brained drama is coming out of Washington, D.C., to roil the markets this week.

For many people, big-yield investing simply means addressing the needs of your own personal situation without being distracted by all the noise (and there is a lot of noise—from a lot of sources—impacting the market).

Just know if you're investing in big yields, you may be sacrificing some long-term price appreciation potential—which may be perfectly fine, depending on your own unique situation.

So with that backdrop in mind, let’s get into the three top ideas and a discussion about whether or not they're worth considering for investment.

Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs)

If you rolled your eyes at the mention of REITs, you're not alone. The group as a whole has performed terribly (as compared to the S&P 500, +188%) over the last decade (see table below) as interest rate volatility has been high (courtesy of increasingly emboldened monetary and fiscal policies following the tech bubble bursting, then the great financial crises, and more recently the pandemic bubble’s meteoric rise and then fall thanks to runaway inflation as rates bounced hard off an artificially low rate near 0.0%).

(SPG) (CCI) (VICI) (ARE) (WPC)

Further still, REITs have suffered as the economy has shifted to more work from home (less demand for office real estate) and online shopping (less demand for shopping malls), to name a couple (dramatic impacts to mortgage rates also slammed mortgage REITs hard as their dependence on leverage and borrowing rates has roiled many of those businesses more than once—for example, see the negative 10-year dividend growth rates for mortgage REITs in the following table).

(NLY) (STWD) (RITM) (ABR)

And from this group of REITs, one very popular standout name is AGNC Investment Corp (AGNC).

1. AGNC Investment Corp, Yield: 16.0%

To be clear, mortgage REITs (such as AGNC) are very different than property REITs. Specifically, where property REITs own mainly physical real estate properties, mREITs own real estate securities (such as those issued by US federal government agencies to fund home ownership).

Mortgage REITs generally offer higher yields than property REITs, but they also come with unique risks (such as higher leverage, or borrowed money).

In the case of AGNC, this mortgage REIT invests mainly in Agency Mortgage Backed Securities, which are considered particularly safe (they’re guaranteed by US government-sponsored agencies). However, AGNC uses significant leverage (recently 7.2x) to magnify the otherwise low returns on these securities, which also magnifies the risks (particularly when interest rates get choppy or when credit spreads widen).

As you can see in our earlier tables, AGNC (and other mREITs) offer attractive double-digit yields, but they also have a history of declining prices and dividend cuts over time.

So if you're going to invest in mREITs, be aware of the dividend and price dynamics (which are totally fine to some investors who just want the outsized dividends, despite the potential for declines over time), whereas others are turned off by the price declines and relatively lower long-term total returns.

A lot depends, of course, on market conditions and valuations (such as price-to-book values, as you can see in our earlier table, which are somewhat attractive - sub-1.0x multiples - following the latest market "flash crash").

However, considering the high-leverage, history of dividend cuts, and ongoing interest rate uncertainty (if rats change faster than expected, it can destroy the effectiveness of mREIT interest rate hedges), some investors may want to consider non-mREIT opportunities (whereas others, more short-term focused, may want to consider mREITs, such as AGNC, for a short-term "buy-the-dip" play).

As such, however, you might consider less risky property REIT names, such as Realty Income (O), the monthly dividend company, which still offers a decent yield (6.1%, higher than normal), trades at a compelling price-to-funds-from-operations (P/FFO) valuation (it’s a value stock/REIT), and has a very long history of actually increasing its dividend. But of course, it depends on your need for current income vs. total returns.

One additional caveat with REITs, however, is that most of their dividends are not qualified (meaning they are taxed at your ordinary income tax rate, instead of the lower qualified rate—similar to the lower capital gains rate). So keep this in mind, depending on how REITs compare to your other investments and whether you're owning them in your taxable investment account or a tax-advantaged retirement account. At the end of the day, it's the after-tax take-home dividend income that usually matters more.

Business Development Companies (BDCs)

Another completely different category of big-yield investments are BDCs. This group makes money by providing capital (mainly loans) to small (middle-market sized) business that pay higher rates on the loans (considerably higher than the 10-year Treasury rate, for example) because they are riskier businesses. However, by building a group of these loans, across a diversified portfolio of small businesses, BDCs can reduce risks and thereby pass along high dividend yields to you—the investor.

(ARCC) (OBDC) (MAIN) (HTGC) (OCSL)

Worth mentioning, BDCs also use a little bit of leverage (or borrowed money) to magnify the interest rate on the loans they make. For example, BDCs can borrow at relatively lower interest rates (because they are considered safer) than the companies they are lending to (at higher rates) because they are riskier. BDCs profit off the spread between these two rates (which is magnified by prudent leverage) and pocket the difference. Worth mentioning: BDCs use a lot less leverage than mortgage REITs (discussed earlier).

Technically, BDCs are required to pay out the majority of their would-be net income as dividends (to investors) in order to avoid being taxed. This is an incentive created by congress to help small business industries, and it also benefits high-income-focused investors too.

You can see (in our earlier table) the recent performance and dividend yields currently offered by top BDCs.

2. Capital Southwest Capital (CSWC),Yield: 13.6%

CSWC is a big-yield (double-digit) middle-market lending firm (it’s a BDC) focused on supporting the acquisition and growth of middle-market companies across the capital structure. Based in Dallas and with only 33 employees, it's internally managed (this is important) and it has total balance sheet assets of around $1.8 billion.

Importantly, Capital Southwest has a strong history of creating value for investors through both a healthy net asset value and strong, growing dividend payments.

CSWC also has an investment-grade credit rating (Baa3 from Moody's and BBB- from Fitch) which is better than the companies it lends to, but also a benefit of being a diversified BDC with a strong balance sheet (and loans heavily tilted towards first lien, safer, at 98%).

From a valuation standpoint, the shares are down over the last month (as investors react to net valuation write-downs of $0.54 cents per share to the debt portfolio, as several of its 118 portfolio companies were moved to tier 3 and 4 ratings, despite steady new deal flow continuing), and the shares now trade at only 1.1x book value, which is low by historical standards for this healthy internally managed BDC (see historical book value range in our earlier table).

If you have been waiting for a pullback in share price and price-to-book-value (“NAV”) for this healthy, long-term BDC, the recent market price declines provide you with some additional margin of safety.

Closed-End Funds ("CEFs")

CEFs are another category of big-yield investments that can be particularly attractive to income-focused investors. They come in a wide variety of shapes and sizes (ranging from bond CEFs to stock CEFs, for example), and they offer the benefit of immediate diversification because they own a lot of individual investments (e.g. stocks and/or bonds).

(DNP) (UTG) (PTY) (GOF) (ADX) (USA)

CEFs also use leverage (or borrowed money) to magnify returns. This can be a very good thing, if done so prudently, but it also introduces risks, particularly when the stock or bond markets sell off. Notably, CEFs use a lot less leverage than mortgage REITs (they’re somewhat similar to BDCs in the leverage department).

CEF Expense Ratios: Some investors despise CEFs because they appear to have high expense ratios. But in many cases, the very high rates can be worthwhile considering a huge portion of it is simply interest paid on the leverage, or borrowed money (plus, they get you access to a lot of “institutional securities” that you could not access on your own). For perspective, BDCs can have similarly large interest expenses, but it's often hidden to investors because it's reported differently (as an operating / financing expense in the financial statements), so just know the high expense ratios on select BDCs can be completely acceptable—despite the initial sticker shock some investors may experience.

After all, every publicly traded stock (with any debt on its balance sheet) is leveraged too. It’s just that investors can view it very differently when it's broken out as an "expense ratio") on a CEF, for example.

You can see many of the top BDCs in our earlier table, and you can review the important questions to ask yourself before investing in a CEF, below.

3. PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO), Yield: 12.5%

When it comes to closed-end funds, PIMCO is the premier industry leader, and its Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund stands out right now for big, steady, well-covered, monthly income and its reasonable price premium for a top PIMCO fund.

In addition to its very attractive monthly income payments, this fund also has risks investors should consider (such as leverage, expenses, price volatility, interest rate risks and the potential for ongoing returns of capital, as we recently wrote about in great detail here).

But at the end of the day, if you're looking for a big steady income, PDO is absolutely worth considering.

The Bottom Line

Not all big yields are created equally. And depending on your own personal situation, some of them are actually worth considering (especially Capital Southwest, certain REITs and select PIMCO CEFs, as highlighted in this report).

And considering the recent "flash crash" in big-yield investment prices, the names in this report are decidedly more attractive than they were at higher prices.

Just understand that if you own big-yield investments—you may end up sacrificing some long-term price appreciation potential (as offered by certain zero-dividend growth stocks), but you're also giving up some extreme volatility and sleepless nights (a good thing for many) as you focus mainly on the steady big income (instead of the short term—often stressful—price volatility).

We continue to share all of our top big-yield investment holdings at Big Dividends PLUS. And disciplined, goal-focused, long-term, high-income investing continues to be a winning strategy.

data.title: Facing Down The Dollar Bear
data.description:
This year’s sharp rise in uncertainty should be a boon for the safe-haven US dollar. Yet, the dollar is not behaving as expected. Why has the dollar sold off and is further depreciation likely?

Typically, the US dollar is considered the ultimate safe-haven currency. This year’s sharp rise in both political and economic uncertainty should be a boon for the greenback. Yet, the dollar has not been behaving as expected, particularly in the wake of Trump’s bombshell reciprocal tariff announcement.

Why has the dollar sold off? And more importantly, is further depreciation likely?

At the start of this year, the US real effective exchange rate was at its highest level since October 1985 (see Exhibit 1). Although the dollar has sold off since, it remains around 15% to 20% above its long-term average.

The dollar has been supported by high US growth relative to the rest of the world, attractive foreign exchange (FX) carry, and strong relative performance of US equities in recent years. Going forward, we expect significant growth convergence between the US and other G10 economies. Under a more benign global scenario, it is quite plausible that over the next one to two years, both the US and eurozone economies will grow by around 1.5%. That would be a marked difference from the past few years, during which the US economy consistently outperformed Europe by 1.5% to 2% per year. This convergence of growth will ultimately reduce USD's FX carry advantage. At the same time, given downside risks to global growth, we believe European currencies and the Japanese yen are likely to benefit more in the near term, including versus emerging market currencies.

Trump’s sweeping reciprocal tariffs could turbocharge this growth convergence. When combined with other growth-negative policies, like aggressive immigration restrictions and government spending cuts, these tariffs increase the risk of a US recession materially.

We see several reasons why the recent selloff may extend to a full-blown dollar bear market. The US may be limited in its ability to offset the growth-negative impact of tariffs. An uptick in inflation expectations caused by tariff uncertainty is likely to keep the Federal Reserve on hold in the near term. Fiscal tools also may be limited given the US’s significantly larger government debt-to-GDP ratio versus its peers.

Meanwhile, developed market economies with lower government debt-to-GDP ratios have more fiscal room to offset the negative impact of tariffs. Additionally, foreign investors retain other tactical tools. Global investors are structurally overweight US asset markets after 15 years of US equity outperformance and USD appreciation. This imbalance is an additional source of vulnerability for the USD. Global investors have both economic and political reasons for reducing US allocations and increasing FX hedge ratios. These shifting dynamics will play out in the months and quarters to come. Other regime changes, like Germany’s massive defense and infrastructure spending plan, will unfold over the next decade, keeping US growth and dollar exceptionalism under pressure.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: IRadimed Corporation: Steady, Low-Risk Grower In The Devices Space
data.description:
IRadimed Corporation (NASDAQ:IRMD) is a small company developing, manufacturing and selling a set of two MRI-compatible products, from which it earns a nice little revenue stream. The company offers a steady growth in revenue, hitting 14 straight quarters of record revenue, and also offers a small dividend to its shareholders. After a long period of steady price appreciation, the company’s stock price is seeing a decline since about February, which has become a little too prominent now. Is this a buy opportunity, or are there headwinds we need to understand?

What IRMD does

IRMD sells two products - the MRidium 3860+ MRI Compatible IV Infusion Pump System, and the IRadimed 3880 MRI Compatible Patient Vital Signs Monitoring System.

IRMD is the only known provider of a non-magnetic IV infusion pump which is FDA 510(k) cleared for safe usage during MRI procedures characterized by a strong magnetic field. Rival infusion pumps may contain metallic parts as well as electronic parts that emit electronic signals which can potentially interfere with the working of the MRI machine. The company’s patented

“MRidium® MRI compatible IV infusion pump system has been designed with a non-magnetic ultrasonic motor, uniquely designed non-ferrous parts and other special features to safely and predictably deliver anesthesia and other IV fluids during various MRI procedures.”

The other product is the MRI compatible patient monitoring system. This, too, has been designed using non-magnetic components keeping the MRI environment in mind. The device is compact and lightweight, and can be carried along with the patient from the CCU to anywhere in the MRI scanner room. Things included in the device: “wireless Electrocardiogram (“ECG”) with dynamic gradient filtering; wireless blood oxygen saturation monitoring (“SpO2”) using Masimo® algorithms; non-magnetic respiratory carbon dioxide (“CO2”); invasive and non-invasive blood pressure; patient temperature; and optional advanced multi-gas anesthetic agent unit featuring continuous Minimum Alveolar Concentration measurements.”

The company notes that its founder and CEO, Roger Susi,

“is a pioneer in the MRI compatible medical device industry, having invented the first MRI compatible patient monitoring system in 1986 and the first non-magnetic MRI compatible IV infusion system in 2004.”

Competitors and addressable market

While the infusion pump has no competitors, at least not among publicly traded companies, the monitor has a few. The infusion pump used to have Bayer as a competitor, but that company stopped selling its pumps in 2015 due to regulatory issues, making IRMD the only seller of these infusion pumps. Real competition comes from B. Braun, Fresenius Kabi and MIPM Mammendorfer Institut für Physik und Medizin, makers of the Faraday Boxes used as stop gap measures in the absence of MRI compatible infusion pumps.

For example, there is the SCHILLER's MAGLIFE RT-1, which as an MRI compatible monitor offers

“10 vital parameters at a glance: ECG, SpO2, NIBP (standard); IBP (optional); etCO2, anaesthetic agents, O2, N2O (optional); spirometry (optional); optical temperature measurement (optional).”

However, IRMD emphasizes its compact and lightweight design that gives its device a competitive edge over larger and heavier machines that may create “workflow issues.”

Besides Schiller, there is also GE HealthCare (GEHC), which is a competitor, but the key competitor, according to the company, is Invivo Research, founded by Roger Susi, IRMD’s own founder. Invivo Research is currently owned by Koninklijke Philips N.V. (PHG).

To help us understand how the company’s infusion pump is utilized, the company gives us 5 scenarios that a facility without such a pump faces. These facilities:

“(1) do not offer MRI treatment to such patients; (2) use standard (magnetic) pumps with long IV lines that extend outside the MRI scanner room; (3) proceed and accept patients for an MRI procedure, but stop the flow of IV fluids during the procedure; (4) allow the gravity controlled free drip of IV fluids; and (5) attempt to shield a conventional IV infusion pump.”

There are not only inefficiencies but obvious hazards from the magnetic and electric current generated in the MRI scanner room. Shield boxes or Faraday cages are inefficient, may hinder operational flow, and still be ineffective. The company’s MRI compatible infusion pump is a must-have item in the MRI room.

The company does not market its safety devices

“in countries that [they] believe do not have a minimum level of patient safety standards to warrant a device like ours.” That is a somewhat startling marketing strategy, and it limits their addressable market to the 27,350 approved MRI scanner facilities available in countries with “acute care facilities of sufficient sophistication.”

For the infusion pump, the company primarily makes money from new customers, but for the monitors, they say it is “more subject to replacement cycles than new adoptions.”

The company makes about 80% of its revenues from U.S. markets and the remaining from international sales. It makes 70% of its revenues from sale of devices, while 30% is made from disposables, services and warranties.

Revenue and financials

IRMD reported the 14th consecutive quarter of record revenue in Q4, achieving $19.4mn in total revenue, an increase of 11% from the same period in 2023. Gross margins were 76.1%, slightly down from 76.9% in Q4 2023, but still very competitive against industry standards. The company expects $78 million to $82 million revenue for the full-year.

There was a slight increase in US sales, which increased by 21% to $16.5 million, accounting for 85% of total revenue. However, there was a corresponding dip in global sales, which decreased by 24% to $2.9 million, indicating challenges in the global markets. Some of these challenges come from the less expensive products available in the Asian markets. For example, Kellymed, a Chinese company, manufactures an MRI compatible infusion pump called the KL-8052N Infusion Pump. This costs around $355. IRMD’s MRidium 3860+ MRI Compatible IV Infusion Pump System costs $3995, or more than 10 times the Chinese device.

The price difference comes from various factors. IRMD’s devices are FDA approved, and that is an expensive process. IRMD’s devices are also premium brands, and have sophisticated features others may not be able to match. However, for care facilities on a tight budget, these Chinese copies offer a cheaper solution, and that eats into IRMD’s topline.

Moving on, operating expenses for the company were $9mn, which is 46% of revenue, and is $1mn more than the previous year, primarily because they increased their sales force slightly. GAAP diluted earnings per share (EPS) was $0.40 (Miss by -$0.01), while non-GAAP was $0.44, which was in line.

In the previous quarter, their infusion pump revenue increased by 34% to $7.6mn, reflecting strong demand and its unrivalled status in sophisticated markets. For the monitor, there was a slight decrease in revenue, to $6.57 million from $6.85 million in Q4 2023.

The company is constructing a new facility, which will be completed by mid-2025 and facilitate growth.

The company also increased its dividend slightly, to $0.17 per share from $0.15, demonstrating confidence.

However, despite these many positives, IRMD stock experienced a sharp decline of approximately 9.44% in pre-market trading following the earnings announcement on February 13. This could be due to investors concerns related to the decrease in international sales. The decline has been sustained during the last two months, possibly reflecting industrywide concerns due to the Trump tariffs and other regulatory issues.

Financials

IRMD has a market cap of $634mn and a cash balance of $52.2mn. R&D expenses were $600,000 while G&A expenses were $8.4mn. Their CFO for the quarter was $6mn, and capex was ($2.8mn), giving them a positive FCF of $3.2mn, and therefore, an indefinite cash runway.

Also note:

For the three months ended December 31, 2024, our free cash flow, a non-GAAP measure, was $2.9 million, down from $3.3 million for the same period in 2023. This decline is related to our ongoing capital expenditures for construction of our new building, which were $2.7 million for the quarter. As Roger noted, we expect to complete the new facility by June and we'll spend approximately another $5.5 million to complete the project.

Risks

Primary risks are lack of fresh R&D and the stock price decline. The company has two products, but these are legacy products, and without fresh innovation, the competition may catch up. Also, the price decline, whether it be for declining international sales or for any other reason, should be a worry for investors.

The company does have a new pump under FDA review - the 3870 MR IV pump. However, “given the turmoil with various agencies in the current administration, it's anyone's guess if the FDA may be operating more slowly than usual,” and the new pump approval may get delayed.

Bottomline

IRadimed Corporation is a solid small company with a steady revenue stream and sober, dependable, consistent revenue and financials. People interested in a steady, virtually risk-free increase in value may buy IRMD at currently depressed prices.

data.title: UiPath: ARR Growth Is Unlikely To Accelerate In The Near Term
data.description:
Investment Overview

I initiated UiPath Inc. (NYSE:PATH) previously with a buy rating, as I was very bullish about the potential of its RPA solution and the value proposition it brings to users. I had the view that annual recurring revenue [ARR] growth will accelerate as the demand environment recovers. Unfortunately, I was wrong on multiple fronts. The macro environment did not get better, and PATH financials continued to show weakness with no signs of stabilization. As such, I am downgrading to a hold rating.

Challenging Macro Environment

Firstly, I was wrong about the direction that the macro environment was going to move towards. My current view is that the macro environment is just brutal, and it is likely to only get worse. While I cannot say for certain the long-term upsides/downsides of President Trump’s tariff plan, I think the near-term impacts on business confidence are huge. The uncertainties of these tariffs (whether they will go up? Or down? Or even get rolled back in the next administration) are likely to freeze up enterprise budgets as management awaits better clarity. Inflation isn’t showing signs of coming down either, and the Fed has trimmed its 2025 GDP growth forecast down to 1.7% from 2.1%, which tells us that they’re expecting real economic pain this year. These are really bad developments for PATH, as it is not a top priority in enterprise investment plans (more like a growth investment), and hence, even with a strong product, without enterprises putting in dollars to invest in growth, ARR is unlikely to see any form of re-acceleration.

Net-New ARR Weakness Indicates Poor Revenue Growth Ahead

I believe the impact of the macro developments has shown up in PATH’s numbers. ARR growth has decelerated for the 12th consecutive quarter, with 4Q25 only growing by ~14%. Net-new ARR was even down by ~31% y/y, a steep deceleration from the -20% y/y seen in 3Q25 and -27.1% y/y seen in 2Q. This is bad, but it is even worse if we consider that management mentioned earlier this year that they saw 2025 as the potential year of stabilization (the market reacted positively to that, and the share price went up to ~$15). I believe the market’s expectations have been reset negatively because of the 4Q25 performance and management’s poor FY26 ARR guidance that calls for $1.816 to $1.821 billion. If you do the math, this implies just $152 million in net-new ARR for the year, down 25% y/y, and marks the fourth consecutive year of decline.

The net-new ARR is probably the most important leading indicator of growth in recurring revenue business, and with it continuously declining at a rapid pace, I simply cannot remain bullish about the likelihood of a re-acceleration in the near term.

Customer Retention and Expansion Metrics Continue to Soften

The weakness is also present in PATH’s existing customer base. The key metric to look at is dollar-based net revenue retention [DBNRR], which reflects expansion activity among existing customers. This metric has fallen from >120% to just 110% in 4Q25, and while it is still positive (anything >100% is positive expansion), the overall number of customers has fallen on a y/y basis for every single quarter in FY25 (down 40 on an absolute basis to 10,750). Remember that I noted there is a long-term growth potential (~26% growth CAGR from 2023 to 2033), and I still think this growth potential is true. But if the number of customers is declining, it is a strong signal that the current demand environment is not simply great.

Two aspects that led me to think a recovery will happen eventually

I totally agree with the bears today that PATH is not a great investment. The macro is bad, and demand has clearly weakened, which has shown up in the numbers. But I wouldn’t totally write off PATH’s ability to re-accelerate growth sometime down the road. There are two things to note. Firstly, I believe the slowdown in the federal vertical is cyclical and not structural. According to the 4Q24 earnings call, the slowdown is tied to procurement delays from political transitions and not from churn or downsizing. Even better, management explicitly called out that the Fed sees the PATH solution as a viable path to meet DOGE’s goals. To me, this feels like demand is still present, but it is taking time to flow into PATH’s books. Therefore, while Federal was cited as a key contributor to the 4Q25 ARR weakness, I see the potential for a rebound eventually as the procurement cycles normalize.

So, Bryan, what I would add, if you go back to our investor conference, it's our third largest vertical, across the company. And just to add a little bit more flavor, in some of the cases, there are moratoriums on procurement for new contracts. So, while something may be funded, they're working through reconciliation bills, overall things, and they're just working through the transition. And that is really changing some of the procurement processes.

Again, we're in constant touch with them, our customers there. Everybody feels good about the value. Actually, they see us as a real avenue to meet a lot of DOGE's goals. But we have to give the appropriate space to let them work through the transition and continue to demonstrate our partnership and value. Company 4Q25 earnings

The second area that gives me confidence in recovery is the progress PATH is making around its Agentic automation platform, with tools like Agent Builder, Test Cloud, and Agent Orchestration set for general availability by late April. I think this could cause a major narrative shift in PATH’s equity story, changing from just an RPA player to a platform that “controls” AI agents. The idea is that the world is going to use more AI agents to automate workflows, and while that significantly increases productivity, enterprises still need a solution to manage all these AI agents. Given that PATH was already an effective tool to manage bots that automate workflows for enterprises, I think it is in a very good position to lead on this front.

If I am right, this could be a huge growth catalyst in the long term, especially given the size of the overall market. Unfortunately, this is not going to drive growth acceleration in the near term, as management noted FY26 to be mostly about proof-of-concepts, with material adoption only showing up in FY27.

Valuation

I no longer see the upside to PATH shares in the near term. My assumptions previously were that growth would accelerate to >20% by FY26, but looking at the developments so far, I don’t think this is remotely possible. Management guidance itself is already pointing to just 7% y/y growth. My new model now expects high-single-digit percentage growth through FY27, with PATH generating only ~$1.64 billion in revenue in FY27. This poor growth profile does not justify PATH trading at where I modeled previously—5.5x forward revenue. Instead, I think it makes more sense for it to trade at where it is today, at 2.5x, a discount to its past three-year trading average, given the sharp deceleration in growth.

Conclusion

I downgrade PATH to a hold rating. The macro headwinds have meaningfully reduced my confidence in a near-term reacceleration. PATH financials also did not show any signs of acceleration either. I do think there are growth catalysts, but until they start to translate into ARR growth acceleration, I wouldn’t rush to turn bullish. Per my model, I think the stock is valued at what it is worth today. Hence, I’m staying on the sidelines.

data.title: Box, Inc.: Path To Growth Acceleration On Track
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Box, Inc. (NYSE:BOX) in Dec’24, in which I recommended a buy rating due to my expectation that BOX’s fundamentals remain sound, and I saw the potential for growth to accelerate to 15%, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. In this update, I continue to believe BOX is on the right track for path acceleration, especially with its Agentic AI product and expanding enterprise share wallet.

Investment Thesis

There are a lot of worries about the macro environment today, and I think it’s a fair concern. However, I think one should still focus on the fundamentals of a company and hold a longer-term view to take advantage of any mispricing today. For BOX, I remained with the view that growth will accelerate to my expected 15% level in the coming years.

There are a couple of reasons why I remain bullish, and the first is the shift toward Agentic AI solutions. I would not go into all the details since management has shared a lot of detailed information during the Analyst Day, but I would like to highlight that BOX’s Agentic AI strategy is not just a generic AI assistant. The distinction here is that BOX’s Agentic AI are agents that can autonomously execute complex tasks (like risk assessments, onboarding documentation, and RFP responses). This is unlike the simple AI agent that helps to summarize text, handle Q&A support, write emails, etc. As such, the value proposition greatly differs, and I believe there will be strong demand as the uplift in productivity is a lot more than those achieved with simple AI agents. To make adoption easier, I also liked that BOX has plans to roll out a no-code tool that allows customers to build Box Apps that integrate with AI agents.

What I think makes this even more bullish is BOX’s monetization strategy. Instead of relying on per-seat pricing, BOX will introduce AI Units—a consumption-based model that bills customers based on how much AI processing they actually use (basically, consumption is calculated via a token method). Although this monetization strategy is more volatile than per-seat pricing, I think it is the right model because I expect the consumption to grow at a rapid pace over time, and BOX will be able to benefit from this ramp-up. To give a sense of the growth contribution, I refer to management estimates, which call for revenue growth of 30% CAGR and expansion from ~5% of total revenue today to >10% over time.

The question is whether BOX can successfully monetize its Agentic AI strategy, and I think the answer is yes. It is apparent that customers are finding value with BOX’s AI solutions, as seen from the several case studies noted in the recent earnings call: One enterprise deployed Box AI across 100,000 internal users; another processed >1 million documents to automate compliance workflows; and a travel tech company reported saving >800 hours by leveraging AI agents for itinerary processing. Although we don’t really know the monetization rate yet (Box AI is available in all plans, but only premium capabilities are being monetized), remember that BOX is seeing a 20-40% uplift in pricing (I noted this before) when they migrate customers from Enterprise Plus to Enterprise Advanced (Advanced is the suite with monetized AI solutions). So, my inference is that users are willing to pay for AI solutions.

Enterprise Suite Penetration Continues to Rise

The second reason to remain bullish is that BOX continues to deepen its enterprise footprint. Take the recent quarter for reference: BOX reported that 87% of deals >$100,000 included Suites, up from 81% in 4Q24, and that Suites is now 60% of total revenue vs. 55% last year. Clearly, enterprises are using more of BOX’s solutions, and this has two key implications. The first is obvious: BOX gets a higher wallet share, which means revenue growth. The second, which, I think, is more important, is that BOX becomes stickier since it is now even more embedded into these enterprise workflows. This gives BOX even more justification to expand wallet share over time since BOX is already addressing a portion of the workflow, and note that the workflows BOX is in are complex, so switching out is not easy once BOX is embedded; hence, it also gives BOX more pricing power.

FY26 Guidance Signals a Reacceleration Underway

As such, while BOX FY26 guidance was not the greatest—guiding for just 6% y/y revenue growth and 7% y/y billings growth—I viewed it positively as it does represent a step up from the 5% growth in FY25 and indicates early signs of reacceleration. I would also highlight that FY26 is not a year of “normalized demand” because of the current macro situation. My opinion is that a lot of enterprises are treading cautiously and may not be willing to go aggressive on investments yet until there is clarity on the macro direction. Against this backdrop, if BOX can accelerate growth (albeit minor), I see it as a big win. Over time, as BOX monetizes its AI strategy and continues to expand its wallet share, I think an acceleration to mid-teens percentage revenue growth is doable.

Valuation

Relative to my previous model, BOX has performed as expected, achieving 5% y/y growth in FY25 and is expected to grow 7% in FY26. I don’t see major fundamental changes; in fact, I think the timeline to acceleration could be faster than I expected if BOX can monetize its AI solutions better than I thought. But for conservatism's sake, I am not going to model that in. I am sticking to my previous view that BOX can accelerate revenue growth back to mid-teens by FY29. By then, the macro environment should be a lot clearer, and BOX AI solutions, monetization, and sales strategy will have been more mature. When this acceleration happens, I believe the market will re-rate BOX multiple upwards, as I expected previously, to 5x forward revenue because of the better growth profile.

Risk

The primary risk is that AI monetization fails. While I am bullish on the use cases and the early adoption that BOX has seen, we still do not know how much revenue AI has contributed. For all we know, BOX may not be able to monetize these solutions, and this could meaningfully impact the timeline of growth acceleration. Microsoft has been investing in its Copilot AI agent solution, and it is still a relatively generic AI agent solution. If Microsoft decides to pursue complex use cases, BOX may not be able to differentiate effectively.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for BOX remains a Buy. While near-term revenue growth is still modest, it is still in line with my expectations, and I think the early signs of AI monetization and expanding enterprise adoption will drive growth acceleration eventually. In particular, if BOX delivers on its Agentic AI roadmap, I believe growth will accelerate to mid-teens over the next few years—and when that happens, the stock’s multiple should rerate upwards accordingly.

data.title: Eastman Chemical: A Likely Trade War Loser
data.description:
Shares of Eastman Chemical (NYSE:EMN) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 24% of their value. Most of the chemical sector has been challenged by relatively weak industrial demand and excess supply, and EMN has faced some of these pressures, particularly in autos. The recent market sell-off caused by tariff fears has worsened the situation, and EMN is likely to be a loser from this policy shift. I last covered shares in February 2024, rating EMN a “sell,” and since then, they have lost 8%, underperforming the market. I remain cautious.

Eastman had begun to see some stabilization in its markets, aided by recovery in aviation as production improves. In 2024, Eastman earned $7.89 in adjusted EPS. As you can see below, volumes and prices rose a bit in Q4, with pricing improving over the course of the year. Thanks to this and strong cost management, EMN enjoyed 190bps of EBIT margin improvement to 13.8%

Looking to this year, EMN was guiding to $8.00-$8.75 of EPS and $1.3 billion of operating cash flow. Idiosyncratically, EMN is benefiting from the ramp of its Kingsport facility, which should provide at least $75 million of incremental EBITDA. Absent this, earnings would be about flat relative to last year, under its guidance. This outlook was based on end markets that would be generally flat. Autos and construction remain headwinds, while aviation is the strong spot.

However, it is essential to emphasize that Eastman’s guidance did not include major tariff headwinds. This is reasonable as two months ago, we did not know what tariff policy would be, and clearly, the Administration’s tariff policy is much harsher than most predicted. EMN’s business also did not have much impact from the first round of China tariffs in the first Trump Administration, though at that time, manufacturing was in a stronger position in 2018 than it is today.

Ultimately, the proposed tariff regime is likely to be a much larger headwind for the company. This earnings season, I anticipate many companies will either cut guidance, or more likely pull guidance altogether. With so much uncertainty over the policy outlook, it is extremely difficult to make a twelve-month forecast—tariffs could go up further or be negotiated away, for example.

Tariffs will have multiple impacts on the economy. To the extent they cause a benefit, there will be two factors. First, companies could reshore to the U.S. This industrial buildout, if it occurs, would likely increase demand for many of EMN’s products used in construction. However, I do not view a surge in construction as likely over the next few months. There is simply too much policy uncertainty to see companies greatly increasing cap-ex budgets.

It takes time to build a factory, so companies need to believe these tariffs will be permanent to make the cost of moving production worthwhile. At this moment, it is hard to know what the long-term tariff policy stance will be. On some countries like China, tariffs feel like a more permanent policy, given Biden maintained the Trump tariffs, but on others like Taiwan, it seems less likely the next Administration would maintain them. If there ends up being a re-shoring tailwind, it is likely more than 6–12 months away.

Next, tariffs can help make domestic producers more profitable by increasing the cost of foreign competition. In the chemicals sector, the challenge has largely been from China. China’s construction activity has slowed considerably from several years ago, and it has built up significant production, causing gluts in products like PVC, for instance, which has weighed on companies like Olin (OLN). However, as EMN noted on its last earnings call, its products do not have much competition from Chinese imports. This is unlikely to be a tailwind for Eastman.

In fact, EMN operates in markets where it is an exporter, aided by cheaper U.S. natural gas prices, rather than facing imported competition. This is my primary concern as to how tariffs can be a major headwind. As you can see below, EMN gets about 40% of its revenue from inside the United States, 12% from China, and nearly half from the rest of the world.

However, its asset footprint is about 80% in the U.S. and just 20% is international. EMN does not serve global markets and produce locally. Rather, it produces primarily in the U.S. and sells abroad, aided by lower natural gas prices in the U.S. vs LNG-dependent markets like the EU, China, and Japan.

As a net exporter, EMN will see little benefit from tariffs on imports into the U.S., but it will be impacted by retaliatory tariffs placed on U.S. products by other countries. China has announced its tariffs, but most of the world is waiting. With the full “reciprocal” tariff not coming into effect until April 9th, many are likely waiting until then to see if they can negotiate a delay. However, some additional tariffs are likely to be announced if Trump implements U.S. tariffs as expected.

These tariffs will erode much of Eastman’s cost advantage, and that will likely reduce its exports. This headwind will exist, even if global economic conditions remained as they were. In reality, conditions will likely worsen as tariffs raise costs. For instance, EU carmakers will likely sell fewer cars into the U.S., reducing their chemical needs. On top of this, EMN will likely see some share losses to these carmakers, given retaliatory tariffs.

This will be a “double-whammy” for the business. I would also note that I expect the auto sector outlook to worsen, as Trump’s 25% tariff on cars is separate from reciprocal action, and I expect this sectoral tariff to remain in place regardless of the course of action on reciprocal tariffs. This will be a headwind for EMN’s results.

The impact of tariffs and slower growth on EMN’s international business is likely to be 5-10% of revenue, while I see its U.S. business facing a 0-5% headwind from businesses reducing discretionary spending amidst economic uncertainty. All in, I see about $500 million of revenue at risk, creating at least a ~$2 risk to EPS. This would not be without precedent; EMN earnings have moved at least $1.75 year-over-year five times in the past decade.

If we see a sharper than 1% recession as a result of tariffs, which is not my base case but a definite risk, EMN earnings could fall harder. Now, I would note that EMN has a strong balance sheet, which will enable it to weather a downturn. It has $837 million of cash and just $4.2 billion of net debt. As such, I view its 4.4% dividend yield as very secure, though I expect it to reduce buybacks from the $300 million done last year. While it generated $700 million of 2024 free cash flow, EMN is likely to generate less than $450 million over the next year.

Calendar 2025 earnings will be somewhat aided by Q1 being before tariffs were implemented, but over the next twelve months, if tariffs remain, and we see moderate levels of retaliation, I see substantial earnings risk and see less than $6.25 of earnings power and ~$450 million of free cash flow. Given the significant hit to business confidence, I do see a risk that this forecast could prove optimistic. That said, we may see some negotiations that reduce the tariff impact, so I view ~$6.25 as a reasonable central case scenario.

Even after shares’ recent decline, EMN is about 12x earnings with a ~5% free cash flow yield. For a commodity chemicals company that may have just lost its primary cost advantage supporting exports (as tariffs offset lower energy costs), this is not a compelling level. Given the significant uncertainty, I see fair value closer to 10x earnings and expect shares to trade into the mid-$60s. With a 15% downside and a business model significantly exposed to higher global tariffs, I would remain a seller of Eastman, unless one expects a quick and favorable outcome to the spiraling trade war.

data.title: Emerging Markets: Policy Uncertainty Tempers A Strong Start To 2025
data.description:
Emerging markets stocks have outperformed developed markets to start 2025, led by a surge in China tech and Brazilian commodities. Macro shifts, AI innovation, and policy support shape the outlook across regions.

Emerging market equities are off to a strong relative start in 2025, with the MSCI Emerging Markets IMI Index rising approximately 1.7% during the first quarter, outperforming developed markets after years of lagging. This performance has been largely led by a rebound in China’s equity markets, particularly in the technology sector. In contrast, India has seen moderate declines amid profit-taking and softening economic data. Brazil and parts of the CEEMEA region have also delivered solid gains, supported by improved macro conditions and investor sentiment. Global trade policy shifts, evolving interest rate expectations, geopolitical developments, and currency dynamics continue to shape the environment for emerging markets.

At the onset of the second quarter, the U.S. administration’s proposed reciprocal tariff framework introduced an additional layer of complexity into the global trade and growth equation. While the scope of the proposed measures is significant - particularly for export-oriented economies in Asia and Europe - it may also serve to reinforce important structural shifts already in motion. Chinese exports to the U.S. now face significant additional cumulative tariffs, presenting short-term challenges but also strengthening the case for continued domestic stimulus and a strategic pivot toward innovation and consumption. India, while targeted in the proposal, remains anchored by robust internal demand drivers supported by demographic, digitization, and policy reform tailwinds. In Europe, potential trade frictions come alongside a renewed emphasis on coordinated fiscal expansion. While the near-term landscape remains fluid and we need to continue to monitor developments closely, we believe these developments ultimately support our focus on selectively identifying resilient, domestically oriented growth opportunities across emerging markets.

China: Rebound Driven by Tech & Policy Support

Chinese equities have surged approximately 15% in the first quarter of 2025, driven by renewed fiscal stimulus, improving investor sentiment, and a notable recovery in large-cap technology stocks. Beijing’s pro-growth policy pivot has been central, with measures to support domestic consumption, stabilize the property market`, and encourage capital market activity. The government’s efforts to restore confidence in the internet sector, highlighted by regulatory easing and supportive rhetoric, have also played a key role. At the same time, breakthroughs like Deepseek’s recent AI advancement underscore the sector’s ongoing innovation and global competitiveness. However, challenges persist. Consumer demand remains uneven, and tensions with the U.S., including hefty proposed tariffs and ongoing export controls on semiconductors, present potential headwinds. While policy execution remains a focus, China’s long-term outlook is underpinned by its push into strategic industries like AI and advanced manufacturing, as well as still-attractive equity valuations following several difficult years.

India: Near-Term Pullback Amid Strong Long-Term Fundamentals

India’s markets have pulled back modestly in early 2025, with the MSCI India Index declining approximately 4% during the first quarter. After a strong multi-year run, elevated valuations, weaker-than-expected GDP growth, and muted corporate earnings prompted foreign investors to trim exposure. Contributing factors include persistent inflation, regulatory tightening, and a slowdown in infrastructure spending ahead of elections. Despite these near-term headwinds, the team’s multiple visits to India in recent months reinforce our conviction in the country’s structural story. Growth drivers such as rising domestic consumption, manufacturing tailwinds from the “Make in India” initiative, and a globally competitive technology services sector remain firmly in place. In our view, the recent correction presents an opportunity to add to high-quality names at more reasonable valuations, with long-term fundamentals intact.

Brazil: Strong Start Amid Commodity & Policy Tailwinds

Brazilian equities have been among the top performers in emerging markets this year, with the MSCI Brazil Index up approximately 15% in U.S. dollar terms. Improving fiscal signals from the government, along with a supportive commodity backdrop, have helped restore investor confidence. Markets have responded positively to more disciplined rhetoric around government spending and tax reform, while rising demand for exports such as iron ore and soybeans—especially from China—has supported earnings. However, the outlook remains sensitive to inflation dynamics, central bank policy, and political developments. The Brazilian real has shown signs of stabilization following sharp depreciation last year, but remains a watchpoint. Despite these risks, Brazil’s diversified economy, attractive equity valuations, and exposure to structural trends in agriculture and financial technology position it well for long-term growth particularly as the country nears the end of its rate hiking cycle.

Technology: EM Tech Stocks Fueling the Rally

The technology sector has been a key contributor to emerging markets’ performance year to date. Gains have been led by Chinese internet firms with optimism around DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), along with easing regulatory risks, driving an acceleration of AI investments and innovation in China. Select semiconductor manufacturers in Taiwan and South Korea are also set to benefit from accelerating demand tied to artificial intelligence. However, risks remain, particularly around U.S.–China technology tensions and export controls that could disrupt key supply chains. Still, the long-term growth trajectory for EM tech remains compelling. Structural themes such as digital transformation, e-commerce penetration, and government-led innovation initiatives are expected to drive sustained investment opportunities across the region.

CEEMEA: Policy Stability And Geopolitical Rebalancing

CEEMEA equities delivered a mixed performance year to date, shaped by a range of idiosyncratic and macro factors across the region. Turkey began the year on a strong footing, with the return to orthodox macroeconomic policy boosting investor confidence. However, the recent arrest of a key opposition figure weighed on sentiment, serving as a reminder of the persistent political risk premium. Central Europe performed well, with Hungary and Poland both seeing gains in equities and currencies. Optimism around the potential for a ceasefire in Ukraine, combined with expansionary fiscal policy in Europe — particularly Germany’s push for growth-oriented spending — supported risk appetite in the region. While potential U.S. tariffs could pressure Europe’s export sectors, fiscal stimulus and domestic investment are creating opportunities for selective bottom-up ideas.

In the Middle East, performance was more muted, but our recent on-the-ground meetings in Saudi Arabia and the UAE leave us constructive on the outlook. Continued structural reforms, government-led investments, and pro-growth agendas are supporting business sentiment and medium-term growth expectations. That said, oil prices and evolving geopolitical dynamics remain key watchpoints for the region.

Macro Landscape

Several global macro trends are shaping the outlook for emerging markets. The proposed expansion of U.S. tariffs - now targeting a broader set of trade partners across Asia and Europe - adds to global uncertainty and raises risks of retaliatory measures. These dynamics could disrupt supply chains and place upward pressure on inflation, complicating the outlook for interest rates. At the same time, they may accelerate structural shifts already underway, including nearshoring, supply chain diversification, and increased focus on domestic demand. Emerging markets continue to benefit from a more dovish U.S. Federal Reserve, which has paused its tightening cycle and signaled potential rate cuts - supporting capital flows and easing currency pressures. A weaker U.S. dollar has further improved debt-servicing capacity and commodity-linked revenues. While volatility in rates and FX remains a risk—particularly for countries with external imbalances—those with sound fundamentals, credible policy frameworks, and room for targeted stimulus are well-positioned to navigate this environment and potentially outperform.

Fund Performance

The VanEck Emerging Markets Fund (the “Fund”) performed in line with the MSCI EM IMI Index on the quarter-to-date basis ending March 31, 2025 (+1.97% for the Fund; +1.70% for the Index). Positive relative performance for the quarter was driven by allocation and stock selection in Brazil, as well as allocation towards Georgia. Negative relative performance was driven by stock selection in China.

China and Brazil were the Fund’s top contributors for the quarter.
